name: Hlin
description: Hlin is a Norse goddess whose name means "protector" or "coverer". She is one of Frigg's handmaidens and is tasked with protecting those Frigg wishes to shield from danger. Hlin listens to the prayers and pleas of mortals and reports them back to Frigg, who then decides whether or not to intervene. She is a goddess associated with comfort, protection, and consolation in times of need. While not featured prominently in many surviving myths, her role as a protector and a close confidante of Frigg underscores her importance within the Norse pantheon. Some interpretations suggest she is an aspect of Frigg herself, representing the goddess's protective and nurturing qualities. Hlin's power resides in her ability to hear and relay the concerns of mortals to Frigg, acting as an intermediary between the human realm and the divine. She embodies the concept of divine intervention and the hope that the gods are listening to the suffering of humanity. Her association with Frigg, the goddess of marriage, motherhood, and home, further emphasizes her role as a protector of the family and the vulnerable. Ultimately, Hlin represents the compassionate and protective side of the divine, offering solace and hope to those in need. She is a symbol of the unseen forces that work to safeguard individuals and communities from harm. Her existence assures mortals that their cries for help do not go unheard, and that the gods are capable of extending their protection to those who seek it.
subtitle: Protector and Consoler of Mortals

keyMyths.title: Hlin's Protection
keyMyths.description: Hlin listens to the prayers of a mother whose child is in danger and relays the message to Frigg, who then intervenes to save the child. This showcases her role as a protector.

keyMyths.title: Secrecy and Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Hlin is entrusted with secrets by Frigg, demonstrating her loyalty and importance within Frigg's inner circle. She is a trusted confidante.

keyMyths.title: Frigg's Companion
keyMyths.description: Hlin is mentioned as being in the company of Frigg, solidifying her position as a handmaiden and close associate of the powerful goddess. This illustrates her close relationship with the queen of the Aesir.

cultural.modernLegacy: While Hlin doesn't have a widespread modern following, she is sometimes invoked by modern Heathens and Asatruar seeking protection and comfort. Her name and role also appear in modern fantasy literature and games inspired by Norse mythology, keeping her memory alive in contemporary culture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Hlin are somewhat obscure, as she is not prominently featured in the surviving Norse texts. She appears primarily in the *Prose Edda* and *Poetic Edda*, where she is mentioned as one of Frigg's handmaidens. Her name, meaning "protector" or "coverer," suggests her primary function. Some scholars propose that Hlin may have been a later addition to the Norse pantheon, perhaps evolving from a more localized protective spirit or a hypostasis of Frigg herself. Given the fragmentary nature of our knowledge about Norse religious practices, a complete understanding of Hlin's early development remains elusive. The lack of specific birth narratives or creation myths associated with Hlin further contributes to the mystery surrounding her beginnings.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Hlin's role is primarily defined by her association with Frigg. She acts as a messenger and intermediary, listening to the prayers of mortals and conveying them to Frigg. The most notable story involving Hlin appears in the *Prose Edda*, where Snorri Sturluson mentions her in connection with Ragnarok. It is stated that Frigg will suffer a "second sorrow" after the death of Odin, which some interpret as referring to Hlin's inability to protect the Allfather from his fate. Other than this, there are no extensive myths directly featuring Hlin as the central figure. Her importance lies in her supportive role and her embodiment of protection and consolation, rather than in independent heroic deeds or dramatic narratives. Her influence is subtle but significant, highlighting the importance of divine aid in Norse cosmology.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Due to the limited textual evidence, the specifics of Hlin's worship are largely unknown. It is plausible that she received veneration as part of Frigg's cult, with offerings and prayers directed towards the queen of the Aesir and her retinue. Given her association with protection and comfort, individuals facing hardship or danger may have invoked Hlin's name in hopes of receiving Frigg's favor. However, there is no direct archaeological or literary evidence to confirm dedicated temples or festivals specifically honoring Hlin. It is likely that any worship of Hlin was integrated within the broader framework of Norse religious practices, which often involved household rituals, personal devotion, and community gatherings focused on the major deities. The lack of surviving records makes it challenging to reconstruct the precise nature of her cult, leaving room for scholarly interpretation and conjecture.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hlin's legacy primarily resides in her symbolic representation of protection and consolation. While not a major figure in Norse mythology, her name and role have endured through the centuries, appearing in various literary and artistic works inspired by Norse mythology. In modern times, Hlin is sometimes invoked by practitioners of Heathenry and Asatru, who seek her assistance in times of need. Her name also lends itself to poetic kennings and metaphors, representing the comforting and protective aspects of the divine feminine. Furthermore, the character of Hlin appears in contemporary fiction and games, often depicted as a loyal and compassionate handmaiden of Frigg, continuing to embody her core attributes of safeguarding and solace. Her presence in popular culture ensures that the essence of Hlin, the Norse goddess of protection, remains relevant in the modern world.

shortDescription: Hlin is a Norse goddess and handmaiden of Frigg, known as a protector and comforter. She shields those Frigg wishes to save from danger.
